digest.top_line: HYDRA (7-RCT IPD meta-analysis): dose-escalated moderate hypofrac adds late G2+ GI/bowel-QoL with no PFS gain vs conventional; isodose 60/20 stays clean.
digest.tldr: Prostate fractionation carried the day: HYDRA warns dose-escalated moderate hypofrac buys late bowel toxicity with no PFS gain (isodose 60/20 stays clean), while modern 5fx SBRT is now NCCN-endorsed for UIR at 1-2% toxicity. In breast, SUPREMO cut 10yr chest-wall recurrence (HR 0.45) but with no DFS/OS gain and RNI prohibited.

sites.intro: Fractionation dominated: a MARCAP IPD meta-analysis on moderate hypofrac plus a review cementing 5fx SBRT for unfavorable intermediate-risk.

studies.name: HYDRA
studies.tldr: No PFS difference vs conventional RT for either MHFRT type, but dose-escalated MHFRT alone raised late G2+ GI + bowel-QoL decrement; isodose 60/20 clean.
details: 🔍 IPD meta-analysis, MARCAP consortium: 7 phase 3 RCTs of CFRT vs MHFRT, IPD obtained from all seven
🔍 Two prespecified strata: 3454 pts (3 isodose trials), 2426 pts (4 dose-escalated trials)
📐 Median f/u 5.4y isodose (IQR 4.6-7.2), 7.1y dose-escalated (5.7-8.4)
📊 CFRT vs MHFRT by stratum (all comparisons vs conventional fractionation)
📊 Bowel toxicity signal confined to the dose-escalated stratum, on both physician (G2+ GI) and patient (QoL) scoring
📊 Isodose MHFRT clean across GU, GI, and both QoL axes
🔗 CFRT control required modern dose (≥70 Gy in 2 Gy equivalents); trials with substandard control excluded
⚠️ Dose-escalated stratum pools heterogeneous fractionation schedules; specific doses not stated in source
⚠️ Late toxicity physician-scored (grading subjectivity), but PRO corroborates the bowel signal

verdict.rationale: IPD meta-analysis of 7 phase 3 RCTs consolidating isodose 60/20 as standard MHFRT; reinforces current practice, no efficacy gained by dose-escalating.
verdict.audience: Localised prostate cancer, definitive EBRT, moderately hypofractionated

studies.significance: The split that matters: dose-escalated MHFRT raised late G2+ GI (OR 1.48, p=0.0035) and patient-reported bowel decrement (OR 1.68, p=0.023), while isodose 60/20 stayed clean on every toxicity and QoL axis at equal PFS. Escalating buys no efficacy, so default to isodose MHFRT.
studies.monday_clinic: In localised prostate cancer choosing moderate hypofractionation, this supports isodose 60 Gy/20 fx over dose-escalated hypofractionated schedules; it does not speak to SBRT/ultrahypofractionation or node-positive disease.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: IPD meta-analysis of **7 phase 3 RCTs** (MARCAP consortium) of CFRT vs MHFRT. Primary efficacy endpoint **PFS**; co-primary toxicity late **G2+ GU and G2+ GI**; co-primary PRO urinary/bowel QoL decrement. Two prespecified strata: isodose vs dose-escalated MHFRT.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: Localised prostate cancer. CFRT arm required modern dose (**≥70 Gy in 2 Gy equivalents**); trials lacking published IPD efficacy or late-toxicity data excluded. **3454 pts** across 3 isodose trials, **2426 pts** across 4 dose-escalated trials.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Isodose MHFRT exemplified by **60 Gy in 20 fractions**; dose-escalated MHFRT schedules pooled (specific fractionations not given in source). Median follow-up **5.4 y** (isodose) and **7.1 y** (dose-escalated).

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: Efficacy equivalent to CFRT for both strata. The only toxicity separations were in the **dose-escalated** arm, late G2+ GI and bowel QoL, not isodose (see table).

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Reinforces isodose 60/20 (CHHiP-type) as the standard MHFRT regimen and argues against dose-escalating hypofractionated schedules, which buy **no efficacy** but cost bowel toxicity.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents localised prostate cancer treated with definitive EBRT where the control arm used modern-dose CFRT. Does not represent SBRT/ultrahypofractionation, brachytherapy, or node-positive/post-operative settings.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Physician-scored late toxicity carries grading subjectivity, though PRO data corroborate the bowel signal. Dose-escalated stratum pools heterogeneous fractionation; longer follow-up needed to confirm late-toxicity divergence.

open_questions: Late-toxicity divergence with follow-up beyond 7 years
Whether ultrahypofractionation (SBRT) shares the dose-escalation toxicity penalty
Optimal dose-escalated schedule if used in higher-risk disease

The main questions it aims to answer are:

* Does reducing the radiation dose to healthy prostate tissue lower the risk of bowel and urinary side effects?
* Can we maintain good cancer control by keeping a high dose for MRI-visible…

studies.name: ASTRO 2024: SBRT for Unfavorable Intermediate-Risk Prostate Cancer
studies.tldr: Modern 5-fraction SBRT (1-2% bothersome toxicity vs 10-30% for 2D-era 45fx) is now NCCN-endorsed for UIR prostate; focal-boost de-escalation is next.
details: 🔍 ASTRO 2024 EDU 16 educational review (Dr. Spratt) on SBRT for unfavorable intermediate-risk (UIR) prostate; not a new trial result.

details.text: 📊 Toxicity has fallen sharply: 2D-era 45fx/9wk carried 10-30% bothersome GU/GI vs ~1-2% with 5fx SBRT over 1-2wk.
📐 RTOG 9408 secondary analysis: UIR prognosis and ADT interaction

details: 🔍 UIR drivers on RTOG 9408: Gleason grade group 3 (HR 3.49), ≥2 intermediate-risk factors (HR 2.40); higher PCSM vs FIR (p=0.013).

details.text: 📐 SBRT dose/fractionation across the reviewed regimens
subdetails: HYPO-RT-PC: 42.7 Gy/7fx (6.1 Gy/fx) vs conventional 78 Gy/39fx (2 Gy/fx)
PACE-B: 5 fx, CTV 40 Gy / PTV 36.25 Gy
Loblaw de-escalation: 35 Gy prostate + 25 Gy pelvis + 50 Gy DIL, 5 fx

details: 🔗 Meta-analysis: pooled late G3+ GU 2.0% (1.4-2.8%), GI 1.1% (0.6-2.0%).
📊 Dose-response: higher SBRT dose → better biochemical control (p=.018) but worse late G3+ GU (p=.014).
⚠️ PACE-B urethra contouring not required (V44Gy<20% only 'if visualized'); many pts likely got ≥48 Gy hotspots (~121 Gy EQD2).
⚠️ That un-constrained urethral dose may explain PACE-B's higher G2+ GU (5.4% SBRT vs 3.7% conventional, p=0.28).
📐 Field direction: de-escalate whole-gland dose while focal-boosting the dominant intraprostatic lesion.
studies.significance: The actionable RT read is urethral dose: PACE-B did not require urethra contouring, so ≥48 Gy hotspots (~121 Gy EQD2) likely drove the higher G2+ GU (5.4% vs 3.7%). Routine urethra delineation plus a V44 constraint, not avoiding SBRT, is what lowers toxicity.
studies.monday_clinic: In unfavorable intermediate-risk localized prostate, this supports 5-fraction SBRT as an NCCN-listed definitive option with urethra contouring; whole-gland de-escalation plus focal DIL boost remains investigational, not standard practice.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: ASTRO 2024 EDU 16 educational session (Dr. Spratt); **narrative review** of SBRT for UIR prostate, not a new trial. Synthesizes RTOG 9408, HYPO-RT-PC, PACE-B and focal-boost series.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: Modern SBRT is **5 fx over 1-2 weeks** (vs 2D-era 45 fx/9 wk). Reviewed schedules: HYPO-RT-PC **42.7 Gy/7 fx**, PACE-B **40 Gy CTV / 36.25 Gy PTV in 5 fx**, focal-boost **36.25 Gy/5 fx + DIL 45-50 Gy**.

analysis_sections.label: Safety
analysis_sections.body: Pooled late **G3+ GU 2.0%** (1.4-2.8), **GI 1.1%** (0.6-2.0). Higher dose improves biochemical control (P=.018) but worsens late G3+ GU (P=.014).

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: PACE-B 5-yr: no EFS difference SBRT vs conventional; G2+ GU **5.4% vs 3.7%** (p=0.28). NCCN lists SBRT for nearly all risk groups including UIR.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: Spratt argues SBRT toxicity reflects **missing dose constraints**, not the modality: un-contoured urethra in PACE-B allowed ≥48 Gy hotspots. Field is moving toward whole-gland de-escalation with a focal DIL boost.

open_questions: Does focal-boost with whole-gland de-escalation improve the therapeutic ratio?
Optimal urethral dose constraint to reduce G2+ GU after prostate SBRT

sites.intro: SUPREMO's no-RNI design leaves the node-positive PMRT question unsettled despite a clean chest-wall recurrence signal.

studies.name: SUPREMO
studies.tldr: 10yr chest-wall recurrence 1.1% vs 2.5% (HR 0.45), no DFS/OS gain, but trial prohibited RNI so tests incomplete PMRT.

details.text: 📊 SUPREMO efficacy: chest-wall RT vs no RT

details: 📊 No DFS or OS improvement from chest-wall irradiation
🔍 SUPREMO tested chest-wall irradiation alone and prohibited regional nodal irradiation
⚠️ Only 25% truly node-negative (majority N1); dilution could mask node-positive PMRT benefit

details.text: ⚠️ Incomplete nodal coverage even in the PMRT arm
subdetails: Internal mammary RT in <2% of pts, both arms

details: 📊 Supplement: LRR benefit concentrated in node-positive pts, absent in node-negative
⚠️ TNBC 10% of cohort; forest plot worse with PMRT, likely underpowering not true harm
⚠️ 65% HR-positive, late-recurrence prone, but only 10-year follow-up reported
🔗 EBCTCG 2014: PMRT cut 10-yr LRR 17.9%, 20-yr breast-cancer mortality 8% in 1-3 node+

verdict.rationale: SUPREMO tested chest-wall RT alone, prohibited RNI, and diluted with 25% node-negative pts, so its null DFS/OS can't establish PMRT omission for node-positive disease.
verdict.audience: Post-mastectomy breast cancer, pN0 or 1-3 positive nodes

studies.significance: Even with SUPREMO's truncated field (chest-wall RT alone, supraclav in just 12%), the supplement shows local recurrence still fell 4.8% to 3.3% (HR 0.51) in node-positive pts. A partial RT field still helping argues FOR nodal irradiation, not omission, in 1-3 node+ disease, so the null OS/DFS shouldn't justify omitting PMRT.
studies.monday_clinic: In 1-3 node-positive post-mastectomy pts, this critique cautions against reading SUPREMO as clearance to omit nodal RT; it does not extend to truly node-negative pts, where SUPREMO and EBCTCG show no PMRT benefit.

analysis_sections.label: Design
analysis_sections.body: ASO editorial (Naoum, Taghian) critically evaluating SUPREMO. SUPREMO randomized post-mastectomy pts to chest-wall irradiation vs observation; **RNI was prohibited**, so it tested chest-wall RT alone, not chest-wall plus nodal PMRT.

analysis_sections.label: Population & inclusion
analysis_sections.body: **Only 25% truly node-negative**; the majority had N1 (1-3 positive nodes). 65% hormone-receptor-positive, 10% triple-negative. Post-neoadjuvant pts are out of scope.

analysis_sections.label: Radiotherapy
analysis_sections.body: **Chest-wall irradiation alone.** Supraclavicular coverage reached only 12% of the PMRT arm (n=97), internal mammary RT under 2% both arms, and regional nodal irradiation was otherwise prohibited.

analysis_sections.label: Results
analysis_sections.body: **No DFS or OS benefit** from chest-wall irradiation; the only positive signal is reduced local recurrence (see table).

analysis_sections.label: vs leading data
analysis_sections.body: Danish and British Columbia trials, plus MA-20 and EORTC 22922, showed regional nodal RT improves LRR, DFS, and breast-cancer mortality in 1-3 node+ disease, even after ALND. SUPREMO's chest-wall-only field never tested this.

analysis_sections.label: Applies to
analysis_sections.body: Represents post-mastectomy breast cancer, node-negative or with 1-3 positive nodes, treated with chest-wall irradiation without full nodal RT. Does not represent post-neoadjuvant patients or those receiving full regional nodal irradiation.

analysis_sections.label: Limitations
analysis_sections.body: Node-negative dilution (25%) may mask node-positive benefit; incomplete nodal field; TNBC (10%) underpowered with a paradoxical forest-plot direction; **only 10-year follow-up** for a 65% HR-positive cohort prone to late recurrence.

analysis_sections.label: Discussion
analysis_sections.body: The editorial's read: SUPREMO's null headline should not license omitting PMRT in node-positive pts, because it never tested chest-wall plus nodal RT. Even its truncated field improved node-positive local recurrence, consistent with the Danish, British Columbia, and EBCTCG data.

open_questions: Full nodal RT vs chest-wall-only PMRT in 1-3 node-positive disease
PMRT value in triple-negative post-mastectomy disease

papers.title: When Postmastectomy Radiotherapy (PMRT) is not Really PMRT: A Critical Evaluation of SUPREMO Trial
papers.note: NOTE: add as a criticism of SUPREMO

papers.title: Hypofractionated radiotherapy for prostate cancer (HYDRA): an individual patient data meta-analysis of randomised trials in the MARCAP consortium.
papers.abstract:
BACKGROUND: Trials comparing moderately hypofractionated radiotherapy (MHFRT) to conventionally-fractionated radiotherapy (CFRT) for prostate cancer have varied considerably in intent (non-inferiority vs superiority) and MHFRT dose. We compare the efficacy and toxicity profiles of isodose MHFRT and dose-escalated MHFRT.

METHODS: This was an individual patient data meta-analysis that identified randomised phase 3 trials of CFRT versus MHFRT that had published individual patient-level data on efficacy and late toxicity. A systematic literature search using MEDLINE, Embase, trial registries, the Web of Science, Scopus, and relevant conference proceedings was initially conducted on Dec 15, 2023, and was re-conducted on Jan 8, 2025. Trials that did not publish efficacy data, did not publish late toxicity data, or did not use modern dose radiotherapy (≥70 Gy in 2 Gy equivalents) in the CFRT group were excluded. Individual patient data were provided to MARCAP by study investigators. Three separate meta-analyses were designed to compare efficacy (primary endpoint was progression-free survival), physician-scored late toxicity (co-primary endpoints were late grade 2 or higher genitourinary and late grade 2 or higher gastrointestinal toxic effects), and patient-reported outcomes (co-primary endpoints were clinically-significant decrements in patient-reported urinary or bowel quality of life) between patients receiving CFRT versus MHFRT.

FINDINGS: We identified 1696 records for review. Seven phase 3 trials comparing MHFRT with CFRT were eligible for inclusion in our analysis. Individual patient data were obtained from these seven studies (3454 patients from three trials comparing CFRT with isodose MHFRT and 2426 patients from four trials comparing CFRT with dose-escalated MHFRT). At a median follow-up of 5·4 years (IQR 4·6-7·2) for isodose MHFRT and 7·1 years (5·7-8·4) for dose-escalated MHFRT, no differences in progression-free survival were detected (hazard ratio 0·92, 95% CI 0·81-1·05; p=0·21 and 0·94, 0·82-1·09; p=0·43 respectively). No increased odds of grade 2 or higher genitourinary toxic effects were identified for either isodose (odds ratio [OR] 1·16, 95 CI% 0·86-1·57; p=0·32) or dose-escalated MHFRT (1·20, 0·95-1·51; p=0·13). The odds of grade 2 or higher gastrointestinal toxic effects were significantly higher with dose-escalated (OR 1·48, 95% CI 1·14-1·92; p=0·0035) but not isodose MHFRT (1·30, 0·59-2·87; p=0·51). Isodose MHFRT was not found to show different odds of urinary quality-of-life decrement (OR 1·03, 95% CI 0·51-2·09; p=0·93) or bowel quality-of-life decrement (0·76, 0·40-1·43; p=0·39). Dose-escalated MHFRT was associated with greater odds of bowel quality-of-life decrement (OR 1·68, 95% CI 1·07-2·61; p=0·023), but no evidence of greater urinary quality-of-life decrement was found (1·57, 0·87-2·85; p=0·13).

INTERPRETATION: Isodose MHFRT and dose-escalated MHFRT both have similar efficacy compared with CFRT, but dose-escalated MHFRT is associated with higher physician-scored and patient-reported bowel toxicity. Isodose regimens, eg, 60 Gy in 20 fractions, should be the standard MHFRT regimen for localised prostate cancer.

FUNDING: None.

papers.title: ASTRO 2024: SBRT for Unfavorable Intermediate-Risk Prostate Cancer
papers.abstract: ASTRO 2024, Prostate Cancer, stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT), external beam radiation therapy (EBRT), image-guided radiation therapy (IGRT), RTOG 9408 (NCT00002597) trial, PACE B trial, FLAME 2.0 trial, FORT trial.
